result.chapter_summary: The educational establishment maintains a 'fortress' of progressive orthodoxy by presenting its hundred-year-old anti-subject-matter ideology as a new 'reform.' Despite dominating teacher training since the 1930s, education professors claim failure is due to poor implementation by 'human vessels' rather than defects in the progressive dogmas themselves.

claims.text: The present era of American education dates from the publication in 1918 of Cardinal Principles of Secondary Education.
claims.source_passage: Historians date the present era of American education from the publication in 1918 of Cardinal Principles of Secondary Education.

claims.text: Cardinal Principles of Secondary Education was an official document representing mainstream educational thought of its time.
claims.source_passage: Written by the Commission on the Reorganization of Secondary Education, and published by the Bureau of Education of the U.S. Department of the Interior, it was an official document that represented mainstream educational thought.

claims.text: The 1918 Cardinal Principles document challenged an emphasis on subject matter in favor of social and vocational topics.
claims.source_passage: Like most subsequent pronouncements of the educational community, it was a reform-minded document that challenged an emphasis on subject matter.

claims.text: The phrase 'command of fundamental processes' was only added to the final version of the Cardinal Principles as an afterthought.
claims.source_passage: The first draft of Cardinal Principles failed even to include the phrase 'command of fundamental processes' (i.e., reading, writing, and arithmetic), which in the final version was the only allusion to academic goals.

claims.text: The educational community's reform proposals are based on the inaccurate belief that a fact-oriented classroom currently prevails in American schools.
claims.source_passage: Their reform proposals continue to be based on a sincere but quite inaccurate belief that a fact-oriented classroom prevails in American public schools today, whereas, in reality, the most striking feature of our elementary schools is that the anti-rote-learning reforms being advocated are already firmly in place.

claims.text: Anti-subject-matter viewpoints have dominated teacher training and certification since the 1930s.
claims.source_passage: The anti-subject-matter viewpoint of Cardinal Principles has dominated the training and certification of teachers in our teacher-training schools since the 1930s, that is, during the entire working lives of all persons now teaching in our schools.

claims.text: Contemporary elementary schools rarely use monologic lectures or insist on the accumulation of facts.
claims.source_passage: Education professors continue to assume that teachers are still giving lectures to docile classes lined up in rows... Yet a visit to any of our public elementary schools will disclose that these denounced practices are rare.

claims.text: There is an extreme disconnection between the perceived evils reformers attack and the actual practices of American elementary schools.
claims.source_passage: The continued beating of this dead horse illustrates the extreme disconnection between the stated evils that are said to need reforming and the actual practices of American elementary schools.

claims.text: By the 1930s, the anti-subject-matter principles of progressive education were the established tenets taught to elementary teachers throughout the nation.
claims.source_passage: Since the publication in 1964 of Lawrence Cremin’s pathbreaking The Transformation of the School, it has been known that by the 1930s the anti-subject-matter principles of progressive education had become the established tenets taught to elementary teachers throughout the nation.

claims.text: Educational reformers consistently treat hundred-year-old doctrines as if they were new innovations.
claims.source_passage: Yet, after many decades of progressivist intellectual dominance, reformers still recommend these doctrines as 'innovations' that are necessary to improve our schools.

claims.text: Education professors blame teachers' implementation rather than their own doctrines for the failure of progressive educational outcomes.
claims.source_passage: Given the stark option of changing one’s cherished doctrines or changing other people’s practices, education professors have tended to prefer the second choice.

claims.text: The persistence of traditional classroom habits, such as orderly rows of desks, is interpreted by progressives as evidence that their dogmas have not been properly put into practice.
claims.source_passage: Teachers and schools just can’t seem to overcome traditional habits. That is, however, just one of the two possible explanations: 1) either the progressive dogmas have not been properly put into practice, or 2) they have been put into practice but are themselves defective.

claims.text: Progressivism has dominated American schools for so long that traditionalist 'rebellions' are proof of the doctrine's impracticality rather than a lack of influence.
claims.source_passage: Progressivism has dominated the schools for so long that small rebellions against it, such as realigning the children’s desks, are less a measure of its lack of influence than its lack of practicality.

claims.text: The true measure of progressivism's influence is the longitudinal decline of subject-matter learning over the last sixty or seventy years.
claims.source_passage: The critical evidence for gauging the influence of progressivism is not a series of snapshots of specific classroom practices, which are highly variable, but a longitudinal view of the decline of subject-matter learning during the last six or seven decades.

claims.text: Anti-subject-matter principles of progressivism have successfully triumphed in American schools.
claims.source_passage: The anti-subject-matter principles of progressivism have demonstrably triumphed in American schools.

claims.text: Learning should be holistic and natural, organized into projects rather than fragmented into isolated academic subjects, according to progressive tenets.
claims.source_passage: Learning should be holistic and natural, presented in projects, not fragmented into isolated academic subjects.

claims.text: Traditional methods of direct instruction and drill actually succeeded in causing children to learn the subjects they were taught.
claims.source_passage: One thing the traditional method of teaching did accomplish was to cause children to actually learn the subjects they were being directly taught and drilled in and then rigorously tested on.

claims.text: Effective teaching requires a mixture of naturalistic and direct instructional methods.
claims.source_passage: Pragmatists like me reject the polar opposition between naturalistic and artificial modes of teaching, and prefer a mixture of naturalistic and more direct instructional methods.

claims.text: The naturalistic method fails because there is no mechanism that ensures 'absorbing' activities lead to actual competencies.
claims.source_passage: The great drawback of the naturalistic method... is that indirection doesn’t always work. No invisible hand guarantees that lively, absorbing school activities will eventuate in the desired learnings and competencies.

claims.text: Futuristic computer enthusiasm is used to divert attention from the failures of progressive 'project-centered' teaching.
claims.source_passage: While we wait for the bookless and brickless classroom to arrive, futuristic enthusiasm for computers serves mainly to divert attention from past failures and perpetuate progressivist thought and practice.

claims.text: The educational concern for 'individual differences' and 'learning styles' is a ubiquitous, long-standing progressive victory rather than a new reform.
claims.source_passage: During the past ten years, I have visited dozens of elementary schools without finding a single one in which sensitivity to the different individual talents and needs of children was not a concern of teachers and principals. This idea is just as firmly established, and as long victorious, as the repudiation of 'rote learning.'

claims.text: Current 'break-the-mold' school reform proposals are actually recycled Progressive-Era concepts from the early 20th century.
claims.source_passage: In 1993, Education Week caught the irony of implying that current reform proposals are new in the following headline about the New American Schools Development Corporation... PROGRESSIVE-ERA CONCEPT NOW BREAKS MOLD: NASDC SCHOOLS EXPLORE 'PROJECT LEARNING'

claims.text: William Heard Kilpatrick was the most influential figure in introducing progressive ideas into American schools of education.
claims.source_passage: Although the progressive movement in American education is often associated with John Dewey or with Harold Rugg... the most influential introducer of progressive ideas into American schools of education was William Heard Kilpatrick.

claims.text: Kilpatrick’s 'Project Method' has been securely in place in American schools for many decades.
claims.source_passage: Kilpatrick’s method, under various names, has long been securely in place.

claims.text: The reform tradition of teaching 'the whole child' rather than focusing on intellectual development remains unbroken.
claims.source_passage: Also unbroken is the reform tradition of teaching the whole child, not just the intellectual side

claims.text: Almost every elementary school in the United States currently practices progressive conceptions like 'creative self-expression' and 'child growth.'
claims.source_passage: Today, it would be hard to find an elementary school in the United States, or for that matter in most nations of Asia or Europe, that does not put into practice at least some of these conceptions.

claims.text: The educational establishment refuses to acknowledge the paradox that schools have not improved despite practicing progressive methods for years.
claims.source_passage: That our schools are neither better nor more equitable, even though these practices have been in place for many years, is a paradox that our educational experts can scarcely admit into contemplation.

claims.text: Modern educational reformers advocate for 'active learning' as a way to empower students against 'unspoken norms' of teacher-led knowledge acquisition.
claims.source_passage: To credit students’ knowledge, and their capacity to construct and critique knowledge, is to empower students in a way that violates the unspoken norms of most classrooms.

claims.text: Current school reform literature is essentially a repetition of the Romantic, progressive literature of the 1910s, 20s, and 30s.
claims.source_passage: There is, in short, little in the current literature of school reform that does not yield a powerful sense of déjà vu to anyone who has read the Romantic, progressive literature of the teens, twenties, and thirties of this century.

claims.text: Changes in educational terminology are merely cosmetic variations of the same long-standing progressive ideas.
claims.source_passage: But except for such cosmetic variations, and an attempt to integrate computers into the mix, the reforms being advocated have long dominated the schools.

claims.text: The educational establishment's claim that there is an 'overemphasis' on subject matter is factually incorrect because the anti-subject matter reform has been victorious for fifty years.
claims.source_passage: The repudiation of the supposedly deleterious 'overemphasis' on subject matter is a reform that has already been victorious for half a century.

claims.text: The most radical reform possible—focusing on well-defined subject matter—is rarely recommended by the educational establishment.
claims.source_passage: The most radical reform that schools could possibly undertake—a focus on well-defined and challenging subject matter in the traditional disciplines—is rarely recommended.

claims.text: Modern educational ideology views traditional subject matter as a target for eradication rather than a goal of schooling.
claims.source_passage: Traditional subject matter is taken to be the very thing that needs to be eradicated.

claims.text: Educational reforms advocated today are merely cosmetic vocabulary variations of ideas that have dominated schools for half a century.
claims.source_passage: But except for such cosmetic variations, and an attempt to integrate computers into the mix, the reforms being advocated have long dominated the schools.

claims.text: A focus on well-defined and challenging subject matter in traditional disciplines would be the most radical reform schools could undertake.
claims.source_passage: The most radical reform that schools could possibly undertake—a focus on well-defined and challenging subject matter in the traditional disciplines—is rarely recommended.

claims.text: The educational establishment views traditional subject matter as an obstacle that needs to be eradicated.
claims.source_passage: Traditional subject matter is taken to be the very thing that needs to be eradicated.

claims.text: An 'antiknowledge attitude' is the defining element in the worldview of many early-childhood educators and reformers.
claims.source_passage: It is not too much to say that an antiknowledge attitude is the defining element in the worldview of many early-childhood educators and reformers.

claims.text: The deep-seated antiknowledge sentiment among educators is a primary cause of American educational failings.
claims.source_passage: Only gradually have I come to realize that this deep-dyed sentiment has been a powerful cause of our educational failings—as serious in its ultimate effects as any other single cause.

claims.text: Educators' dismissals of factual knowledge are expressions of fundamental belief rather than mere strategic rhetorical devices to resist change.
claims.source_passage: I was able to interpret the statements I was hearing as strategic defense mechanisms rather than as expressions of fundamental belief. ... Only gradually have I come to realize that this deep-dyed sentiment has been a powerful cause of our educational failings.

claims.text: Social constraints within the educational community powerfully inhibit dissenters from defending the teaching of factual knowledge.
claims.source_passage: If dissenters were present, they were being powerfully inhibited by social constraints.

claims.text: American educators generally believe that learning specific factual information, such as geography or astronomy, does not contribute to 'becoming a better person.'
claims.source_passage: an educator asked me if I thought that tidbit of information had made me a better person. Again, no one spoke up to defend teaching factual knowledge.

claims.text: The concept of 'developmental appropriateness' is used by educators to justify withholding academic content from young children.
claims.source_passage: Was I aware that it was developmentally inappropriate to expose first graders to the Eiffel Tower—as the Core Knowledge materials recommended?

claims.text: French preschoolers visit the Eiffel Tower without suffering harmful psychological effects.
claims.source_passage: I didn’t dare mention as relevant the fact that troops of French preschoolers continually visit the Eiffel Tower without harmful psychological effects

claims.text: American educators reject international educational comparisons by asserting that American children are fundamentally different from those in other nations.
claims.source_passage: I had already learned that any mention of French children (or those of other nations) would bring the reproach that we are not French.

claims.text: The assertion that exposing first graders to cultural landmarks like the Eiffel Tower is psychologically harmful is false.
claims.source_passage: I didn’t dare mention as relevant the fact that troops of French preschoolers continually visit the Eiffel Tower without harmful psychological effects

claims.text: Educational administrators across the United States share a similar attitude regarding the uselessness of factual knowledge.
claims.source_passage: On each campus, I encountered similar attitudes regarding the uselessness of factual knowledge and the undesirability of asking students to learn it.

claims.text: School officials almost universally assume that teaching facts must occur in a fragmented, dry, and inhumane way.
claims.source_passage: It was almost universally assumed that the teaching and learning of facts must occur in a fragmented, dry, and inhumane way

claims.text: Modern enthusiasm for technology is currently being used to reinforce long-standing antifact prejudices in the educational community.
claims.source_passage: It was at these IDEA meetings that I also realized the degree to which these antifact prejudices are currently being reinforced by an enthusiasm for the “new world of technology.”

claims.text: The 'knowledge explosion' is cited by educators to justify the irrelevance of teaching specific facts.
claims.source_passage: A “knowledge explosion” has occurred that makes the teaching of “today’s facts” irrelevant, since they are sure to be gone tomorrow.

claims.text: Educational experts view technology as a means for reinforcing their antagonism to factual knowledge rather than a reason to increase intellectual capital.
claims.source_passage: Instead of taking the view that the information age requires everyone to have more intellectual capital, these experts viewed technology as a means for reinforcing rather than reducing their antagonism to teaching factual knowledge.

claims.text: Educators maintain a false polarity between 'meaningless facts' and 'true knowledge' which they frame as a moral struggle.
claims.source_passage: In these educators’ minds, there existed a polarity between exciting, practically useful, morally beneficial teaching on the one hand and memorization of meaningless facts through dry, dull teaching on the other.

claims.text: Factually rich and demanding learning of subject matter can be interesting, captivating, and morally beneficial.
claims.source_passage: The possibility that factually rich and demanding learning of subject matter could, while requiring hard work, also be interesting, even captivating—not to mention morally beneficial and skill-enhancing—was not a concept that IDEA participants brought under discussion.

claims.text: It is logically impossible to know the interrelations of things without first knowing the things themselves.
claims.source_passage: some participants said that it consisted in knowing “the interrelations of things,” but they did not explain how things can be related without first being known.

claims.text: Educational administrators incorrectly assume that important factual knowledge is picked up automatically through experience.
claims.source_passage: They also assumed that any really important factual knowledge is picked up automatically in the course of experience. (That it is not automatically picked up by American students is by now well documented.)

claims.text: Educators mistakenly assume that student ignorance is a condition created by the 'larger culture' rather than by the failures of schooling itself.
claims.source_passage: They assumed that our students’ ignorance is a condition created by “the larger culture” rather than a condition created by schooling itself.

claims.text: There is a causal relation between a student's lack of factual knowledge and their inability to read, write, and solve math problems.
claims.source_passage: There was no thought of a possible causal relation between lack of factual knowledge and lack of ability to read, write, and solve math problems.

claims.text: The ideology of 'knowledge plus' is used by school systems to evade accountability for gaps in student learning.
claims.source_passage: The Westport system tries to achieve “knowledge plus,” he said.

claims.text: The educational slogan 'Teach the child, not the subject' has practical consequences that are harmful to student learning.
claims.source_passage: The practical consequence of these hoary slogans, however, are anything but benign, and their continued dominance shows the degree to which even the ablest educators have been captivated by polarities that date back seventy or eighty years.

claims.text: Education schools continue to corrupt the common sense of teachers and administrators by promoting polarized, oversimplified pedagogical slogans.
claims.source_passage: Clearly, Dewey did not succeed in laying the oversimplifications to rest. On the contrary, they continue to be promulgated by education schools, continue to corrupt the common sense of even some of our ablest teachers and administrators, and continue to be advocated by experts under the banner of “reform.”

claims.text: The United States possesses the best public universities but the worst public schools in the developed world.
claims.source_passage: There is wide agreement in the international community that the United States has created the best public universities and the worst public schools of the developed world.

claims.text: It is easier for a nation to create a high-quality K-12 system than a high-quality university system.
claims.source_passage: In fact, it is easier to create a good K–12 system than a good university system, the evidence being that many nations have created better elementary schools than ours but few or none have created better universities.

claims.text: The excellence of American universities is driven by a tradition of free speech, dissent, and iconoclasm.
claims.source_passage: Perhaps American culture is friendly to first-rate universities... because of our tradition of free speech and our consequent toleration, even encouragement, of dissent.

claims.text: The K-12 educational community is characterized by an intolerant and conformist atmosphere.
claims.source_passage: That conception is a universe away from the intolerant, conformist atmosphere of the educational community.

claims.text: The American K-12 system disparages depth, breadth, and accuracy of knowledge as belonging to the 'banking theory of schooling.'
claims.source_passage: But depth, breadth, and accuracy of knowledge are the very things that our K–12 system tends to disparage as belonging to the “banking theory of schooling.”

claims.text: In the K-12 system, knowledge is considered less desirable than all-purpose goals like 'higher-order skills,' 'self-esteem,' and 'critical-thinking skills.'
claims.source_passage: Knowledge is considered less desirable than more “practical” all-purpose goals such as “higher-order skills,” “self-esteem,” “metacognitive skills,” and “critical-thinking skills.”

claims.text: Educationists wrongly conceive of 'mere facts' as being indissolubly linked to 'rote learning.'
claims.source_passage: “Mere facts” are conceived to be indissolubly connected to “rote learning,” which may be the most disparaging phrase in the educationists’ glossary.

claims.text: American colleges and universities place great value on depth, breadth, and accuracy of knowledge, as well as independence of thought.
claims.source_passage: Our colleges and universities, and the scholars who control their destinies, place great value on depth, breadth, and accuracy of knowledge, as well as on independence of thought.

claims.text: The long-term excellence of American universities is threatened by the ill-preparedness of entering students.
claims.source_passage: It is unclear how long our best universities can maintain their excellence when the students who enter them and who will subsequently staff them are ill-prepared.

claims.text: Since 1965, there has been a 75 percent decline in the absolute number of students scoring above 650 on verbal and math college-entrance tests.
claims.source_passage: Since 1965, for example, there has been a 75 percent decline in the absolute number of students who score above 650 in verbal and math college-entrance tests

claims.text: The decline of high-scoring students is not caused by the inclusion of a greater number of minority students in the testing pool.
claims.source_passage: proving that the decline of our best students is not owing simply to the inclusion of a greater number of minority students.

claims.text: American research universities maintain their quality through an influx of well-educated foreign postgraduate students.
claims.source_passage: Helping to maintain quality against this tide of ill-preparedness has been an influx of well-educated foreigners into our research universities.

claims.text: A nation cannot permanently maintain a K-12 intellectual deficit.
claims.source_passage: we cannot permanently maintain a K–12 intellectual deficit any more than we can permanently maintain a negative trade balance.

claims.text: Most elite American universities must now operate remedial centers for writing, mathematics, and reading.
claims.source_passage: All of our most-difficult-to-enter universities must now maintain remedial centers for writing and mathematics, and in some cases for reading.

claims.text: The domestic ill-preparedness of students leads to a decline of standards at all American universities.
claims.source_passage: It is an inherently unstable situation and must lead to a decline of standards at all American universities, and probably has already done so.

claims.text: The quality gap between universities and K-12 schools is evidence that the slogans dominating the K-12 system are defective.
claims.source_passage: The very existence of this quality gap is presumptive evidence that the slogans dominating our K–12 system and the efforts to reform it are defective and do not deserve the benefit of the doubt.

claims.text: Educational reform requires restructuring the ideas of reformers rather than just the schools themselves.
claims.source_passage: It is not mainly our schools that need “restructuring,” but the ideas of those who would restructure them.

claims.text: Non-educationist reformers are consistently defeated by the educational community's use of progressive slogans.
claims.source_passage: Parents, politicians, business executives, and other outsiders have knocked at the gate of a huge Castle... only to be turned aside or caught in the web of powerful slogans

claims.text: Private-sector innovations and philanthropic 'break-the-mold' schools continue to use the same content-neglectful phrases of the educational establishment.
claims.source_passage: But each hopeful “innovation” echoes the familiar content-neglectful phrases: “critical thinking,” and so on. No force has been able to resist this intellectual orthodoxy.

claims.text: Parent empowerment through charter schools and school choice is a sound theory for moderating institutional monopoly.
claims.source_passage: These ideas are based on the sound theory that parent empowerment would moderate the evils of institutional monopoly through the power of competition.

claims.text: Competition alone may be insufficient to improve schools because the economic analogy for educational competition is imperfect.
claims.source_passage: I agree with this idea in theory, but in practice there may be a flaw in assuming that competition alone can greatly improve schools under present circumstances. The implicit analogy with economic competition is imperfect.

claims.text: Parents lack a clear conception of what schools should be doing day-by-day to achieve long-term goals.
claims.source_passage: Even when parents know what they want to be achieved in the long run, they rarely have a clear conception of what they wish schools to be doing day by day in order to achieve it.

claims.text: American parents exhibit a paradox where they believe the nation's schools are failing while believing their own child's school is performing well.
claims.source_passage: American parents think that our schools are failing in general (because they know that the nationwide results are poor), whereas they think that their own child’s school (a clean, well-lighted place where lots of activity is going on) is performing well.

claims.text: The economic analogy of competition is imperfect in education because schooling results take a long time to manifest and consumer preference is not easily determined.
claims.source_passage: The implicit analogy with economic competition is imperfect. In the economic sphere, customers tend to know what they want. Consumer preference in schooling, by contrast, is not easily determined by the consumers themselves. For one thing, the results of schooling take a long time to show themselves.

claims.text: Parental choice has not significantly improved student achievement in the United States to date.
claims.source_passage: The available evidence shows that parental choice has not greatly improved student achievement in the United States to date

claims.text: School choice by itself is not an adequate principle of educational reform.
claims.source_passage: choice by itself is not an adequate principle of reform.

claims.text: The Netherlands, despite having school choice, has the least consistent school quality in northern continental Europe.
claims.source_passage: The Dutch instituted publicly financed school choice many years ago, and today the Netherlands exhibits the least consistent school quality in northern continental Europe.

claims.text: Structural reforms are unlikely to leverage the educational system until the current intellectual monopoly is broken.
claims.source_passage: However, it is unlikely that any structural reform can leverage our whole educational system until the current intellectual monopoly is broken.

claims.text: Parents lack a principled way of judging comparative school quality, often defaulting to proximity as the primary factor in school choice.
claims.source_passage: Since they rarely have a principled way of judging the comparative quality of the close vs. the distant school, they will usually keep their child in place.

claims.text: Meaningful school choice is currently impossible in the US because nearly all schools subscribe to the same educational philosophy and rhetoric.
claims.source_passage: Overshadowing all of these difficulties is another formidable impediment to informed parental choice: the near impossibility of choosing between schools, even those that adopt special themes, when all of them espouse the same general “philosophy” of education

claims.text: The common rhetorical terms used by American schools (e.g., 'critical thinking', 'self-esteem', 'child-centered pedagogy') mask a lack of substantive difference in educational approach.
claims.source_passage: all of them espouse the same general “philosophy” of education—the same concern for “the individual child and his or her needs,” for “critical thinking,” “self-esteem,” “joy in learning,” “respect for others”... With such similarity of rhetoric among schools, it is a discerning parent indeed who can make a wise choice.

claims.text: A school philosophy focused on specific, grade-by-grade core knowledge would be highly attractive to parents if it were actually available.
claims.source_passage: When American parents are offered that kind of choice, there is evidence that they will send their children long distances to attend such a school.

claims.text: Attempts to establish knowledge-focused public schools in the United States are met with fierce resistance from the educational community.
claims.source_passage: any attempt in present-day America to create a public school that focuses on knowledge is usually met with disparagement and fierce resistance.

claims.text: Attaining specific and well-integrated knowledge and skill gives students pleasure in learning and self-respect.
claims.source_passage: Attaining this specific and well-integrated knowledge and skill gives our students pleasure in learning, as well as self-respect

claims.text: American parents are willing to send their children long distances to attend knowledge-based schools when given the choice.
claims.source_passage: When American parents are offered that kind of choice, there is evidence that they will send their children long distances to attend such a school.

claims.text: The best-performing school systems of Europe and Asia are guided by a knowledge-based educational philosophy.
claims.source_passage: Although such a philosophy guides the best-performing school systems of Europe and Asia, any attempt in present-day America to create a public school that focuses on knowledge is usually met with disparagement and fierce resistance.

claims.text: Minority parents view rigorous, knowledge-based education as the foundation for their children's economic autonomy.
claims.source_passage: a disproportionately large number of children of minority parents, who see such rigorous education as the foundation of their children’s economic autonomy.

claims.text: Public education in the United States functions as an institutional and intellectual monopoly.
claims.source_passage: Critics have long complained that public education in the United States is an institutional and intellectual monopoly.

claims.text: The educational monopoly is sustained through its power to certify teachers via education school requirements.
claims.source_passage: The control exercised by this monopoly is sustained by its power to certify teachers.

claims.text: The teacher certification process serves as a captive audience for ideological indoctrination.
claims.source_passage: The millions of teachers who pass through this certification process become a captive audience for indoctrination.

claims.text: Educational administrators and teacher-trainers hold a uniform set of dogmas regarding the aims and methods of education.
claims.source_passage: They constitute a cohesive body of believers with a clearly formulated set of dogmas and doctrines

claims.text: The educational community uses mastery of slogans as a technique to prevent outside interference.
claims.source_passage: the citadel of education has developed powerful techniques for preventing outside interference, not least of which is mastery of slogan.

claims.text: Private foundations have been co-opted by the educational Thoughtworld, hampering knowledge-based school reform.
claims.source_passage: The co-opting of much of the foundation world by this system of ideas has been an especially grave handicap to knowledge-based school reform.

claims.text: The educational establishment portrays its failed ideas as new, research-based, and conducive to social justice when they are none of those things.
claims.source_passage: The old guard has brilliantly succeeded in portraying its failed ideas as new, research-based, and conducive to social justice, although they are none of these.

claims.text: There is an almost complete absence of rigorous internal criticism within the professional educational community.
claims.source_passage: One of the most shocking facts about the field of education is the almost complete absence of rigorous criticism from within.

claims.text: Professional educationists shower external critics with vituperation and personal abuse rather than engaging in honest criticism.
claims.source_passage: the extreme unwillingness of professional educationists to submit their proposals to free public discussion and honest criticism frequently assumes the even uglier form of showering critics, no matter how upright and well-informed, with vituperation and personal abuse

claims.text: The educational citadel has become an institutional monopoly through administrative control over employment and foundation money.
claims.source_passage: When such pressure for intellectual conformity is combined with administrative control over employment and the dispensation of a great deal of foundation money, it is not surprising that the citadel should become an institutional monopoly.

claims.text: Institutional monopoly alone is not the primary cause of the education system's failure.
claims.source_passage: Yet institutional monopoly alone is not the source of the system’s failure.

claims.text: An intellectual monopoly which requires conformity of ideas is more stultifying than a merely institutional/bureaucratic one.
claims.source_passage: Our American experience demonstrates that an intellectual monopoly which requires conformity of ideas is more stultifying than a merely institutional one.

claims.text: The intellectual uniformity of the American educational community was a consequence of the propagation of ideas from Teachers College, Columbia University.
claims.source_passage: From Teachers College, Columbia University, there occurred a quasi-biological propagation of ideas through which the intellectual DNA of the parent institution in New York was implanted in daughter institutions

claims.text: American educational ideas are more extreme and process-dominated than those in more successful international systems.
claims.source_passage: Its prevailing ideas are more extreme and-process-dominated than those found in systems that are more successful than our own.

claims.text: Emphasizing critical thinking while de-emphasizing knowledge reduces a student's capacity to think critically.
claims.source_passage: To stress critical thinking while de-emphasizing knowledge reduces a student’s capacity to think critically.

claims.text: Giving children constant praise regardless of achievement breeds complacency and ultimately causes a decline in self-esteem.
claims.source_passage: Giving a child constant praise to bolster self-esteem regardless of academic achievement breeds complacency, or skepticism, or both, and, ultimately, a decline in self-esteem.

claims.text: Paying significant attention to each individual child in a standard-sized class results in individual neglect for most children.
claims.source_passage: For a teacher to pay significant attention to each individual child in a class of twenty to forty students means individual neglect for most children most of the time.

claims.text: The material now fed to American children under the guise of developmental appropriateness is 'infantile pabulum' that bores students.
claims.source_passage: while the infantile pabulum now fed to American children is developmentally inappropriate (in a downward direction) and often bores them.

claims.text: True educational reform requires that parents and politicians be provided access to well-established alternative expertise.
claims.source_passage: parents, politicians, and philanthropists cannot be expected to exercise skepticism unless they are provided access to well-established alternative expertise—the chief requisite for true reform.

claims.text: Finding the most effective methods for teaching young children how to read is a technical rather than an ideological matter.
claims.source_passage: finding the most effective methods for teaching young children how to read is a technical rather than an ideological matter.

claims.text: A middle-of-the-road approach that includes both phonics and whole-language elements is the most effective teaching method for reading.
claims.source_passage: a middle-of-the-road approach which includes both phonics and a whole-language approach is the most effective teaching method.

claims.text: The 'phonics approach' to reading instruction is incorrectly identified with conservative Republican politics.
claims.source_passage: Yet the “phonics approach” to reading instruction is identified with “conservative,” “hickory stick” Republicans

claims.text: Researchers who support balanced reading instruction have endured rancorous attacks and cynicism regarding their motives.
claims.source_passage: But what rancorous attacks from both sides these researchers have endured, what cynicism about their “hidden agendas!”

claims.text: In mainline departments of psychology, there is a consensus supporting the conclusion that a middle-of-the-road reading approach is superior.
claims.source_passage: In mainline departments of psychology (as distinct from education-school departments of psychology), among those who have studied the subject there is consensus supporting the Chall-Adams middle-of-the-road conclusion.

claims.text: Mainline research findings are ignored by the educational community in favor of ideological polarization.
claims.source_passage: Still, mainline research findings are simply ignored in favor of polarization.

claims.text: The educational establishment uses the tactic of labeling any challenge to progressive orthodoxy as 'politically conservative' to silence criticism.
claims.source_passage: It is assumed that any challenge to progressive educational orthodoxy must be a politically conservative challenge. This tactic has been quite effective in silencing criticism and avoiding changes in practice.

claims.text: Education reporting in the press is generally weak, as the beat is often assigned to junior reporters who leave before gaining sufficient skepticism.
claims.source_passage: Education is usually assigned to a junior reporter, who, just as he or she has gained useful knowledge and skepticism, is elevated to another beat.

claims.text: It is a fallacy to assume that it is possible to teach abstract, generalized problem-solving ability.
claims.source_passage: 2) that it is possible to teach abstract problem-solving ability

claims.text: Generalized skills like 'learning how to learn' do not enable a lifetime of learning without the foundation of specific knowledge.
claims.source_passage: 3) that once provided with these abstract abilities, students would be able to pursue a “lifetime of learning.”

claims.text: The mainstream scientific community gives little credence to the psychological presupposition that children can be taught generalized critical-thinking skills.
claims.source_passage: If they did, they would learn that the mainstream scientific community gives little credence to many of the psychological presuppositions of the educational community, such as the assumption that children can be taught generalized critical-thinking and problem-solving skills.

claims.text: The public needs a 'choice of ideas' in education even more urgently than they need a 'choice of schools.'
claims.source_passage: The public needs to be offered choice of ideas even more than choice of schools.

claims.text: The educational community resists scientific criticism and the 'rough-and-tumble' scrutiny typical of other academic disciplines.
claims.source_passage: The educational community, operating behind a web of slogans and brooking no internal dissent, resists the scrutiny and the rough-and-tumble of scientific criticism that characterizes subject-matter disciplines.

claims.text: The deception of the public by the educational community regarding its 'reform' status is not deliberate; educators sincerely but inaccurately believe a fact-oriented classroom still prevails.
claims.source_passage: But lest it be thought that members of the educational community knowingly pretend to be reformers while they are really status quo traditionalists voicing age-old slogans, it must be conceded that the deception is not deliberate. Their reform proposals continue to be based on a sincere but quite inaccurate belief that a fact-oriented classroom prevails...

claims.text: The anti-subject-matter viewpoint has dominated the training of every person currently teaching in American schools, as it has been the standard since the 1930s.
claims.source_passage: The anti-subject-matter viewpoint of Cardinal Principles has dominated the training and certification of teachers in our teacher-training schools since the 1930s, that is, during the entire working lives of all persons now teaching in our schools.

claims.text: The progressive 'project method' specifically claims to raise student interest, fix learnings vividly in memory, and stimulate creativity.
claims.source_passage: The project method claims to raise student interest, fix learnings vividly in students’ memories, induce love of learning, and stimulate students’ creativity and individuality.

claims.text: There is no 'invisible hand' or inherent logical guarantee that engaging school activities will naturally result in the acquisition of specific competencies.
claims.source_passage: No invisible hand guarantees that lively, absorbing school activities will eventuate in the desired learnings and competencies.

claims.text: Futuristic enthusiasm for computers in the classroom serves primarily as a diversion to maintain progressive practices despite their history of failure.
claims.source_passage: While we wait for the bookless and brickless classroom to arrive, futuristic enthusiasm for computers serves mainly to divert attention from past failures and perpetuate progressivist thought and practice.

claims.text: Parents frequently default to the closest school because they lack a principled method for judging whether a more distant school offers significantly higher quality.
claims.source_passage: Since they rarely have a principled way of judging the comparative quality of the close vs. the distant school, they will usually keep their child in place.

claims.text: The propagation of progressive ideology from Teachers College, Columbia University, to other institutions occurred through a 'quasi-biological' process of replicating intellectual DNA.
claims.source_passage: From Teachers College, Columbia University, there occurred a quasi-biological propagation of ideas through which the intellectual DNA of the parent institution in New York was implanted in daughter institutions...

claims.text: Educational ministries in other successful democracies (like Norway or Japan) are more effective than the US system because they are answerable to legislatures and open to scholarly dissent.
claims.source_passage: Powerful as they are, these ministries are answerable to legislatures, and are peopled by wide-ranging scholars who are open to criticism and new ideas. The educational ministry of Norway is a distinguished case in point.

claims.text: Many educational issues, such as the best method for teaching reading, are technical matters that have been inappropriately politicized as ideological conflicts.
claims.source_passage: One barrier to developing such expertise has been the politicization of educational issues that are at bottom technical rather than political.

claims.text: The lack of skepticism in education reporting is due to the press treating the 'education beat' as low-status and assigning it to junior reporters who move on quickly.
claims.source_passage: reporters tend to consider the education beat the way they do agricultural reporting in the hinterlands—of enormous importance but dull. Education is usually assigned to a junior reporter, who, just as he or she has gained useful knowledge and skepticism, is elevated to another beat.

evidence.description: Cardinal Principles of Secondary Education (1918) manifesto prioritizing health, vocation, and family over academic subject matter.
Lawrence Cremin's historical analysis of the shift to progressive education by the 1930s.
evidence.source_reference: Cremin, Lawrence. The Transformation of the School (1964)

evidence.description: Larry Cuban's observation that educational ideas are like surface waves that do not change deeper school practices.
evidence.source_reference: Cuban, Larry. How Teachers Taught

evidence.description: Arthur Zilversmit's observation that schools continually revert to traditional practices like rows of desks.
evidence.source_reference: Zilversmit, Arthur. Changing Schools

evidence.description: Longitudinal decline in subject-matter knowledge over 6-7 decades.
Historical account of the persistence of traditional habits vs progressive dogma.
evidence.source_reference: Cuban, How Teachers Taught; Zilversmit, Changing Schools

evidence.description: Author's personal visits to dozens of elementary schools over ten years observing universal concern for individual differences.
The massive sales (over 60,000 copies) of William Heard Kilpatrick’s 'The Project Method' in 1918.
Education Week article documenting NASDC's 'break-the-mold' schools as exploring 'project learning.'
evidence.source_reference: Olson, 'Progressive-Era Concept Now Breaks Mold', 1993

evidence.description: The 1918 publication and high sales of 'The Project Method' show the long history of current reform ideas.
evidence.source_reference: William Heard Kilpatrick, The Project Method (1918)

evidence.description: Descriptions of 'The Child-Centered School' (1928) demonstrate that current ideals of freedom and creative self-expression were established decades ago.
evidence.source_reference: Harold Rugg, The Child-Centered School (1928)

evidence.description: Article in Education Week complaining that subject matter reform is a 'magician's trick' and advocating for pedagogy over content.
evidence.source_reference: 'Better Teaching or Just the Facts, Ma’am?', Education Week (1982)

evidence.description: Author's firsthand experience and observation at IDEA conferences in Denver, Appleton, and Los Angeles involving 150-250 school administrators.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch's personal account of IDEA gatherings

evidence.description: The specific rejection by school administrators of teaching first graders the names of the seven continents.
Observation of French preschool behavior regarding cultural landmarks like the Eiffel Tower.
Hirsch's personal interactions and discussions with hundreds of educational administrators at IDEA campuses.
The behavior of French preschoolers visiting the Eiffel Tower as evidence that such exposure is not psychologically harmful.
Ravitch and Finn's documentation showing that American 17-year-olds lack basic factual knowledge despite experience.
evidence.source_reference: Ravitch and Finn, What Do Our 17-Year-Olds Know?

evidence.description: Statements made by Dr. Kelleher, Westport Superintendent, defending gaps in student learning as a result of the 'information explosion.'
evidence.source_reference: Westport (Conn.) News, June 3, 1994

evidence.description: The 'IDEA' participants' assumption that student ignorance comes from the 'larger culture' rather than schooling.
Dr. Kelleher, Westport Superintendent, arguing that the 'information explosion' justifies a 'knowledge plus' approach over specific subject matter.
The 1994 Teacher of the Year stating that teachers are not there to teach subject matter but to 'teach children.'
OECD report 1993-94 evaluating US universities as the best and US schools as among the worst.
evidence.source_reference: Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development, OECD Economic Surveys, 1993–94: United States

evidence.description: International trend where foreign students flock to US universities but well-informed foreign parents avoid US public elementary schools.
Anecdote of a Swiss medical researcher at UVA debating returning to Switzerland due to the poor quality of local public schools.
75 percent decline in students scoring above 650 in verbal and math SATs since 1965.
Japanese high-technology industry roots in US universities, with 13,000 Japanese students in the US vs 700 Americans in Japan.
Proliferation of remedial writing, math, and reading centers at 'most-difficult-to-enter' universities.
Survey data showing parents critique schools generally but praise their own child's specific school.
Comparative data on the percentage of 'below-par' schools in choice vs. non-choice systems (16% in Netherlands vs. 1-5% in Sweden, Denmark, Japan, South Korea).
evidence.source_reference: Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD), 1993-94
evidence.source_passage: Today, some 16 percent of Dutch schools perform below par, compared with 1 to 5 percent of below-par schools in the nonchoice systems of Sweden, Denmark, Japan, and South Korea.

evidence.description: The attempt by parents in Fort Collins, Colorado (1992) to start an elementary school based on core knowledge principles, which was resisted by the local educational community.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch (1996), Chapter 3 Endnote 12
evidence.source_passage: In 1992, for example, a group of parents in Fort Collins, Colorado, tried to start an elementary school on the principles described above. The educational community fought bitterly at every step

evidence.description: The Washington Core Knowledge School in Fort Collins, Colorado, which succeeded despite fierce institutional resistance and maintains a long waiting list.
Arthur Bestor's analysis of the three interrelated professional groups forming an 'interlocking directorate.'
evidence.source_reference: Bestor, Educational Wastelands (1953)

evidence.description: Mortimer Smith's observation of the religious-like uniformity of opinion in education schools and state departments.
evidence.source_reference: M. Smith, And Madly Teach (1949)

evidence.description: The historical shift in foundation leadership from independent critics like James Koerner to Thoughtworld allies.
evidence.source_reference: Koerner, The Miseducation of American Teachers (1963)

evidence.description: Arthur Bestor's 1953 observation of internal educational conformity and the abuse of external critics.
Japan's school system is under tight ministry control (Monbusho) but remains high-quality and fair.
Norway's educational ministry is an example of a distinguished, open-minded institutional monopoly.
The propagation of educational theory as 'intellectual DNA' from Teachers College, Columbia University to other education schools.
Jeanne Chall and Marilyn Jager Adams' research finding balanced reading instruction most effective.
Teachers College professors forced the insertion of a pro-whole-language afterword into Adams' research to maintain ideological alignment.
The consensus within mainline psychology departments regarding reading instruction methods.
evidence.source_reference: Adams, Beginning to Read; Pressley and Rankin (1994)

evidence.description: The specific editorial in the Springfield Sunday Republican that repeated educational fallacies about abstract skills.
evidence.source_reference: Springfield (Mass.) Sunday Republican, January 2, 1994

evidence.description: The instance of Teachers College professors insisting on adding a 'liberal' afterword to Marilyn Jager Adams's book.
evidence.source_reference: Adams, Beginning to Read

concepts.author_definition: A historical euphemism for the basic academic skills of reading, writing, and arithmetic.
An ideological stance within the educational community that de-emphasizes the teaching of specific academic content in favor of social, vocational, or developmental goals.
A pejorative term used by progressives to describe memorization and drill, which Hirsch argues has been successfully but unnecessarily eradicated from schools.
An instructional method where knowledge is organized around a 'big idea' or common principle through student-planned activities, rather than traditional academic subjects.
concepts.common_alternative: Sometimes viewed as a modern innovation, but Hirsch identifies it as a 1918 concept.
concepts.source_passage: ‘project-based learning’ organizes knowledge from a number of disciplines around a big idea or common principle, instead of around more traditional subjects.

concepts.author_definition: Holistic, hands-on teaching of cognitive skills that mimics natural learning processes, opposed to direct 'lecture-and-drill' methods.
The use of computer technology and predictions of 'bookless classrooms' to justify and reinforce progressive antipathy toward structured, book-based learning.
An educational philosophy that prioritizes the physical, rhythmic, and emotional growth of the student over intellectual or subject-matter discipline.
concepts.source_passage: The whole child is to be educated. Hence the materials of education are as broad and interrelated as life itself. For experience is not only an intellectual matter; it is physical, rhythmic, emotional.

concepts.author_definition: A paradigm where students are credited with the capacity to create and critique knowledge themselves, rather than acquiring it from a teacher.
concepts.source_passage: To credit students’ knowledge, and their capacity to construct and critique knowledge, is to empower students in a way that violates the unspoken norms of most classrooms.

concepts.author_definition: Surface-level changes in educational terminology (e.g., 'thematic learning' instead of 'projects') that mask the underlying continuity of progressive ideology.
A pervasive contempt for or aversion to factual information, viewing it as 'trivial pursuit' or 'rote learning' that does not contribute to personal growth.
An ideological construct used by educators to claim certain academic subjects or facts are psychologically harmful or unsuitable for young children, regardless of evidence from other cultures.
concepts.common_alternative: A scientific determination of what children can cognitively handle at certain ages.

concepts.author_definition: The rapid production of information in the modern age, used by educators as an excuse for not teaching specific subject matter because it allegedly becomes 'outmoded' quickly.
A rhetorical dichotomy where 'mere facts' are disparaged as dry and disconnected, while 'true knowledge' is elevated as meaningful and morally beneficial, though often left undefined.
concepts.source_passage: In the IDEA discussions, facts and information were invariably referred to as “mere,” in contrast to something better called “true knowledge.”

concepts.author_definition: A buzzword used by administrators to suggest that schools are providing something more valuable than 'mere' subject matter, often masking a neglect of factual curriculum.

concepts.term: Teach the child, not the subject
concepts.author_definition: A slogan of child-centered schooling that suggests focusing on the student's natural development and experience over the mastery of formal academic disciplines.
An educationist term of disparagement for any curriculum that emphasizes the accumulation of specific knowledge, implying it is passive and rote.
A metaphor for the educational establishment, characterized as an impenetrable, Kafkaesque fortress that resists external reform through ideological jargon.
concepts.source_passage: Parents, politicians, business executives, and other outsiders have knocked at the gate of a huge Castle, as in a Kafka story

concepts.author_definition: The dominant ideological consensus within the educational community that prioritizes skills and processes over content and knowledge.
concepts.source_passage: No force has been able to resist this intellectual orthodoxy.

concepts.author_definition: The dominance of a single ideological framework (romantic-progressive education) across all teacher training institutions and school districts, which prevents structural reforms like school choice from offering truly different educational options.
concepts.common_alternative: Economic monopoly (control of a market by a single firm).
concepts.source_passage: However, it is unlikely that any structural reform can leverage our whole educational system until the current intellectual monopoly is broken.

concepts.author_definition: An educational philosophy where teachers have subject-matter expertise and a school-wide agreement exists on a definite, specific core of knowledge and skills that all children attain in each grade.
A coordinated monopoly of professors of education, local administrators, and government bureaucrats who control educational policy and curriculum.
A technique used by the educational establishment to shield failed ideas from criticism by framing them in appealing, unassailable language (e.g., 'research-based' or 'social justice').
Administrative and bureaucratic control over schooling, including employment and funding, which exists in many nations but does not inherently degrade quality.
Overly simplified and academically weak curriculum materials that are mistakenly labeled as 'developmentally appropriate' for young children.
An instructional method for reading that combines phonics (technical decoding skills) with whole-language approaches (meaning-based immersion).
The ability of the public to choose between competing educational philosophies and evidence-based methods, rather than just choosing between different school buildings (school choice).
The fallacious idea that cognitive skills like reading and problem-solving can be taught as general, content-independent techniques.
The enclosed, self-perpetuating system of progressive educational ideas that resists external criticism and scientific data.
The foundational progressive principles originated at Teachers College, Columbia University, and replicated across all American teacher-training schools.
A term used to describe teachers when they are viewed by education professors as the faulty components responsible for the failure of progressive dogmas.
A futuristic educational ideal where physical schools and printed books are replaced by electronic, hands-on technology.
A branding term used by reformers to suggest innovation, though the author argues these reforms typically recycle 1918-era progressive concepts.

cases.name: The publication of Cardinal Principles (1918)
cases.description: The Bureau of Education of the U.S. Department of the Interior published a manifesto that moved American education away from academic subject matter toward a focus on health, vocation, family membership, ethical character, and leisure.
key_passages: In 1918, of course, schools were still strongly focused on academic subject matter, including Latin. But with ever greater numbers of students coming to school... the time had come... to place a countervailing emphasis on health, vocation, family membership, ethical character, and the worthy use of leisure.

cases.name: NASDC (New American Schools Development Corporation)
cases.description: A 1990s reform group that sought to create 'break-the-mold' schools, which Hirsch points out were actually implementing old progressive 'project learning' ideas from 1918.
key_passages: In 1993, Education Week caught the irony of implying that current reform proposals are new in the following headline about the New American Schools Development Corporation... NASDC SCHOOLS EXPLORE 'PROJECT LEARNING'

cases.name: IDEA Conferences
cases.description: A series of summer gatherings for school principals and administrators where Hirsch observed the professional culture of education. He found a unanimous, socially enforced refusal to defend the teaching of factual content to young children, even when the facts were foundational geography or science.
key_passages: These gatherings, administered by an organization called IDEA, take place during the summer on college campuses in such places as Denver, Appleton, Wisconsin, and Los Angeles.

Immediately, one of the participants asked me if I really thought it was of any use whatever to a first grader to learn the seven continents? No one at the meeting was willing to defend the idea of teaching such facts to young children.

cases.name: IDEA Meetings
cases.description: Hirsch attended several meetings of the Institute for Development of Educational Activities (IDEA) where he engaged with hundreds of school administrators. He observed a pervasive and aggressive hostility toward factual knowledge and a reliance on technological trends to justify that hostility.
key_passages: During the next several days, I traveled to two more IDEA campuses where large numbers of educational administrators were gathered... I encountered similar attitudes regarding the uselessness of factual knowledge
At IDEA, I had entered an either-or world that had the flavor of a medieval morality play, in which good was set against evil.

cases.name: Westport, Connecticut School System
cases.description: A case where a local superintendent (Dr. Kelleher) responded to parental complaints about gaps in learning by invoking the 'information explosion' and the 'knowledge plus' philosophy to justify the school's approach.
key_passages: As a Westport, Connecticut, superintendent recently told parents who complained of great gaps in their children’s learning, the schools were imparting more than facts
Knowing what subject matter to teach is a problem because of the information explosion, said Dr. Kelleher.

cases.name: The University of Virginia
cases.description: Used as an example of the Jeffersonian ideal of 'illimitable freedom of the human mind,' contrasted against the conformist K-12 educational community.
key_passages: Over a portal of the University of Virginia’s Cabell Hall are Jefferson’s words about the kind of university he wished to create: “This institution will be based on the illimitable freedom of the human mind."

cases.name: The US Higher Education System
cases.description: The author contrasts the success of American universities—widely regarded as the world's best—with the failures of the K-12 system to highlight that the issue is not 'American culture' but specific educational ideologies in the K-12 sector.
key_passages: There is wide agreement in the international community that the United States has created the best public universities and the worst public schools of the developed world.

cases.name: Japanese High-Technology Industry
cases.description: Hirsch notes that the Japanese high-tech sector has strong intellectual roots in American universities, evidenced by the large number of Japanese students studying in the U.S. compared to the few Americans studying in Japan.
key_passages: The Japanese high-technology industry, for example, has strong intellectual roots in American universities, with more than 13,000 Japanese students in the United States (compared with about 700 American students in Japan).

cases.name: The Netherlands school system
cases.description: A long-standing publicly financed school choice system. Despite the structural choice, the author claims it results in the least consistent school quality in northern continental Europe, with a high percentage of schools performing below par compared to systems without choice.
key_passages: The Dutch instituted publicly financed school choice many years ago, and today the Netherlands exhibits the least consistent school quality in northern continental Europe.
Today, some 16 percent of Dutch schools perform below par, compared with 1 to 5 percent of below-par schools in the nonchoice systems of Sweden, Denmark, Japan, and South Korea.

cases.name: Fort Collins, Colorado (1992)
cases.description: An episode where parents attempted to establish a school based on Core Knowledge principles. The local educational establishment allegedly used legal threats, intimidation against teachers, and bitter opposition to stop the project.
key_passages: In 1992, for example, a group of parents in Fort Collins, Colorado, tried to start an elementary school on the principles described above.
The educational community fought bitterly at every step, threatening to retaliate against teachers who wished to cooperate, and going so far as to hire lawyers

cases.name: Washington Core Knowledge School
cases.description: A group of parents in Fort Collins, Colorado, founded this school in 1992 based on knowledge-rich principles. They faced bitter resistance from the local educational community, which threatened teachers and hired lawyers to stop them. The school eventually succeeded and became highly popular, particularly among minority families.
key_passages: In 1992, for example, a group of parents in Fort Collins, Colorado, tried to start an elementary school on the principles described above. The educational community fought bitterly at every step, threatening to retaliate against teachers who wished to cooperate, and going so far as to hire lawyers to help restrain parents from promulgating such heresy.
The school has an enthusiastic student body and a long waiting list, which includes a disproportionately large number of children of minority parents

cases.name: Japan's Monbusho
cases.description: Japan's elementary schools are highly effective and equitable despite being under the centralized, monopolistic control of the ministry of education (Monbusho).
key_passages: Japan’s elementary schools are among the best and fairest in the world, yet Japan’s schools are under the tight control of Monbusho, the all-powerful ministry of education.

cases.name: Teachers College, Columbia University
cases.description: The primary source of the 'intellectual DNA' of the American educational monopoly, which has replicated its views through generations of education professors across the country.
key_passages: From Teachers College, Columbia University, there occurred a quasi-biological propagation of ideas through which the intellectual DNA of the parent institution in New York was implanted in daughter institutions

cases.name: Marilyn Jager Adams's book publication
cases.description: Despite being funded by the US Department of Education and providing a scientifically grounded middle-ground approach to reading, Adams's work was subjected to ideological gatekeeping by professors at Teachers College who demanded a pro-whole-language 'Afterword' be included to offset its findings.
key_passages: Indeed, because Adams’s work was sponsored by research grants from the U.S. Department of Education, two education professors from Teachers College, Columbia University, insisted upon inserting in her book a more “liberal,” pro-whole-language “Afterword,” adding to the public confusion.

thinkers.name: Lawrence Cremin
thinkers.key_idea: The shift to progressive education as the established national tenet for teachers was complete by the 1930s.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Cremin's historical dating to prove that progressive ideology is the status quo, not a new 'reform.'
key_passages: Since the publication in 1964 of Lawrence Cremin’s pathbreaking The Transformation of the School, it has been known that by the 1930s the anti-subject-matter principles of progressive education had become the established tenets taught to elementary teachers throughout the nation.

thinkers.name: Larry Cuban
thinkers.key_idea: Educational ideas are like waves on the surface that do not affect the deep, traditional practices of actual schools.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch acknowledges Cuban's view that classroom practice hasn't changed, but suggests it's a framing used to protect progressive dogmas from scrutiny.
key_passages: For Cuban, the movements of ideas in American education are like waves on the surface of the sea, scarcely affecting the depths below.

thinkers.name: William Heard Kilpatrick
thinkers.key_idea: The Project Method as the primary vehicle for progressive education.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Kilpatrick to prove that current 'reforms' are actually nearly a century old.
key_passages: The most widely distributed educational pamphlet of the teens and twenties of this century was William Heard Kilpatrick’s The Project Method (1918)

thinkers.name: John Dewey
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Dewey's own reservations about the naturalistic method to show that even the father of progressivism saw its limitations.
key_passages: The great drawback of the naturalistic method, which critics of progressive 'reforms' [including Dewey] long ago pointed out, is that indirection doesn’t always work.

thinkers.name: Harold Rugg
thinkers.key_idea: The child-centered school, focusing on freedom, activity, and creative self-expression.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Rugg's work to show that 'modern' progressive descriptions of the 'new school' are actually nearly 70 years old.
key_passages: The hallmarks of the new school are freedom, activity, and creative self-expression... The book from which the above recommendations (in quotation marks) were excerpted is The Child-Centered School, published in 1928.

thinkers.name: Dr. Kelleher
thinkers.key_idea: Schools cannot decide which subject matter to teach because of the information explosion; instead, they should focus on 'knowledge plus.'
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Kelleher's statements as a contemporary example of how administrative rhetoric is used to dismiss parental concerns about a lack of content in the curriculum.
key_passages: The Westport system tries to achieve “knowledge plus,” he said.

thinkers.name: Thomas Jefferson
thinkers.key_idea: The foundation of a university must be the illimitable freedom of the human mind to follow truth and combat error through reason.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes Jefferson to illustrate the intellectual openness of universities, which he contrasts with the 'intolerant, conformist atmosphere' of the K-12 educational establishment.
key_passages: Over a portal of the University of Virginia’s Cabell Hall are Jefferson’s words about the kind of university he wished to create: “This institution will be based on the illimitable freedom of the human mind."

thinkers.name: Franz Kafka
thinkers.key_idea: The concept of an unreachable and incomprehensible administrative power ('The Castle').
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Kafka's 'The Castle' as a metaphor for the educational establishment's ability to resist and confuse outside reformers.
key_passages: Parents, politicians, business executives, and other outsiders have knocked at the gate of a huge Castle, as in a Kafka story, only to be turned aside or caught in the web of powerful slogans

thinkers.name: Arthur Bestor
thinkers.key_idea: The 'interlocking public school directorate' comprising education professors, administrators, and government officials.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Bestor's 1953 analysis to argue that the institutional monopoly has only grown more powerful and insulated since the mid-20th century.
key_passages: We owe to Arthur Bestor the phrase 'interlocking public school directorate' to describe the coordinated groups of people who control the institutions of the education world
It is sad to think that there is nothing outdated in his 1953 description of the educational community’s resistance

thinkers.name: Mortimer Smith
thinkers.key_idea: The educational establishment functions like a religious sect with uniform dogmas and a certification process that ensures only believers enter.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Smith to emphasize the 'amazing uniformity of opinion' and the use of state laws to enforce ideological conformity among teachers.
key_passages: Mortimer Smith, observing the similarity between educational thought and the dogmas of a religious sect
They constitute a cohesive body of believers with a clearly formulated set of dogmas and doctrines

thinkers.name: James Koerner
thinkers.key_idea: An independent scholar and critic of the 'Miseducation of American Teachers.'
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch contrasts Koerner's former independence as a foundation officer with the current co-opted state of the foundation world.
key_passages: The time seems to be past when program officers are independent scholars and vigorous critics of the Thoughtworld, such as James Koerner at the Sloan Foundation

thinkers.name: Marilyn Jager Adams
thinkers.key_idea: Balanced reading instruction (phonics + whole language) is the most effective method.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Adams as a victim of the 'citadel's' politicization, noting that her research was undermined by ideologically motivated afterwords from Teachers College professors.
key_passages: In the heat of this battle, few have wished to heed researchers like Jeanne Chall and Marilyn Jager Adams

thinkers.name: Jeanne Chall
thinkers.key_idea: Synthesized evidence showing that a balanced approach to reading instruction is superior to polarized methods.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites her as a primary scientific authority whose work is ignored by the educational establishment due to political polarization.
key_passages: In the heat of this battle, few have wished to heed researchers like Jeanne Chall... who found that a middle-of-the-road approach which includes both phonics and a whole-language approach is the most effective teaching method.

actors.name: Commission on the Reorganization of Secondary Education
actors.context: Author of the 1918 Cardinal Principles document.

actors.name: Education Professors
actors.context: The group Hirsch claims maintains the 'fortress' of progressive ideology and refuses to admit the failure of their dogmas.

actors.name: Education Professors
actors.context: Accused of protecting their doctrines by blaming teachers for the failure of progressive methods.

actors.name: NASDC
actors.context: A reform group used as an example of rebranding old ideas as 'break-the-mold'.

actors.name: Education Week
actors.context: A publication that Hirsch cites both for capturing the irony of 'new' reforms and for publishing 'entirely typical' progressive complaints against subject matter.

actors.name: Teachers College, Columbia University
actors.context: The institution where William Heard Kilpatrick trained a large number of future education professors in progressive ideas.

actors.name: IDEA (Institute for Development of Educational Activities)
actors.context: Organization that administers summer conferences for school principals and administrators.

actors.name: Early-childhood educators and reformers
actors.context: The group Hirsch identifies as possessing an 'antiknowledge attitude' that prevents effective curriculum reform.

actors.name: IDEA (Institute for Development of Educational Activities)
actors.context: The organization that hosted the meetings where Hirsch observed the consensus of educational administrators.

actors.name: Educational Administrators
actors.context: The primary group Hirsch identifies as the 'impregnable fortress' protecting antifact ideologies.

actors.name: Dr. Kelleher
actors.context: Superintendent of Westport, CT, who defended 'knowledge plus' education against parents' concerns about learning gaps.

actors.name: 1994 Teacher of the Year
actors.context: Professional who stated that teachers are there to 'teach children,' not 'subject matter.'

actors.name: OECD
actors.context: International body that evaluated the US as having the best universities and the worst public schools.

actors.name: Educational Community
actors.context: The 'intolerant, conformist' institutional group that protects progressive slogans and resists knowledge-based curricula.

actors.name: Reformers
actors.context: Parents, politicians, and business executives who attempt to improve schools but are defeated by the establishment.

actors.name: American Parents
actors.context: Described as desiring long-term success but confused by short-term pedagogical rhetoric and structural proximity.

actors.name: Fort Collins Educational Community
actors.context: The institutional group that resisted the creation of a knowledge-based school.

actors.name: National Education Association (NEA)
actors.context: Identified as part of the modern expansion of the interlocking directorate.

actors.name: Sloan Foundation
actors.context: Cited as a former home for independent critics of educational ideology.

actors.name: Professional Educationists
actors.context: The collective group of professors, administrators, and officials accused of maintaining the intellectual monopoly.

actors.name: Teachers College, Columbia University
